title: How to Build Relationships With Property Managers (Scripts Included)
dek: Property managers are the most valuable people in a rental agent's world, and most agents approach them exactly wrong: with a pitch. Here's what works instead, with the words to use.
heroAlt: How to Build Relationships With Property Managers (Scripts Included)

blocks.html: A property manager's life is occupancy rates, maintenance tickets, and difficult residents. Agents, to them, are either a reliability (qualified renters, smooth showings) or a liability (no-shows, unprepared clients, wasted time). Your entire strategy is to be unmistakably the first kind.

blocks.text: Managers don't need another agent. They need a reliable one.

blocks.html: Email or a thirty-second call. The script: "Hi [name], I'm [you], a leasing agent working [neighborhood]. I regularly work with renters looking in your area. I'd love to know the best way to work with your team when I have a qualified client: how you prefer showings scheduled, and whether you co-op with outside agents. I keep my clients prepared and my showings on time. What's the best way to stay in the loop on availability?"
Notice: no pitch, one small ask, and the question nobody else asks, which is how THEY prefer to work.

Twice a month, a light touch: "Any upcoming availability I should know about for my pipeline?" A thank-you after every showing. Deliver on tiny promises. Show up early every time. Send a holiday note. None of it is clever. All of it compounds.

You'll know the relationship is real when the flow reverses: they call you. A renter their building couldn't fit. A unit about to hit the market. A resident's friend moving to town. Protect that trust like the asset it is: never oversell their building, never bring an unqualified client, never make them chase you for paperwork.
All the scripts, in sequence, are in my free ebook Luxury Leases, Real Profits. Better agents win.
